body:
Are there good reasons to invest in Aviva? Chief executive Dame Amanda Blanc says the outlook for the insurer has never been better. Do the numbers back up her confidence?  Dame Amanda Blanc declared that “the outlook for Aviva has never been better” last week when she reported third-quarter figures and set out new three-year targets for the FTSE 100 insurer. Is she right and if so, is this optimism reflected in the company’s valuation? Thrust into the top job in July 2020 after a short stint as a non-executive on its board, the insurance industry veteran has already spearheaded a turnaround at Aviva that has earned plaudits in the City.  Aviva was at a low ebb when she took charge, with investors frustrated by its lacklustre stock market returns under a succession of bosses. Blanc quickly took action to address these concerns by selling a string of non-core businesses overseas, including Aviva’s operations in France, Italy, Turkey, Poland, Vietnam and Singapore, a reshaping of the group that generated proceeds of more than £8 billion.  This not only streamlined the insurer by refocusing it on its core markets of the UK, Ireland and Canada, it enabled Blanc to step up capital returns to shareholders, with more than £10 billion handed back over the past five years.  It was a pivot praised by Cevian, an activist investor which revealed in 2021 that it was a big shareholder in Aviva and sold out in 2023, when it praised Aviva for having been “transformed from a poorly performing conglomerate to a focused and well-performing insurance company”.  However, despite Blanc’s programme of disposals and capital returns, it has not simply been a strategy of retrenchment. There have been acquisitions too, including a £385 million deal in 2022 to buy Succession Wealth to expand Aviva’s presence in the wealth management market.  The following year Aviva spent £460 million on AIG’s UK protection business and £100 million on Optiom in Canada, while in March 2024 the company announced it was re-entering the Lloyd’s of London insurance market after 24 years through a £242 million deal for Probitas. All of these deals enlarged Aviva’s capital-light operations, helping it to differentiate it from its more capital-heavy rival Legal & General. Then came Blanc’s biggest gamble yet, with Aviva’s £3.7 billion agreement last December to acquire troubled rival Direct Line. A further expansion of its capital-light business, the takeover, which was finalised in July this year, makes Aviva Britain’s biggest motor insurer.  Aviva had initially estimated it would save £125 million a year by eliminating duplicated roles, digitising Direct Line’s operations and making other cost cuts following the takeover. As was widely expected, however, it significantly increased its cost synergy estimate to £225 million in its trading update last week, when it also said it expected at least £500 million of capital synergies from the deal.   There was other good news. Blanc had been aiming to deliver £2 billion of operating profit in 2026 but Aviva said it would hit this target a year early, with the group expecting to generate £2.2 billion in 2025. Even stripping out the contribution made by Direct Line this year, Aviva’s operating profits are still forecast to surpass £2 billion.  The insurer also set new goals for 2028, saying it was targeting a return on equity of more than 20 per cent and aiming to grow adjusted earnings at an average annual rate of 11 per cent over the next three years.  The turnaround Blanc has already achieved has powered Aviva’s share price higher. Its stock closed on Wednesday evening at 632p, up 134 per cent since she was appointed, although it has dipped in recent days amid some disappointment with its third-quarter numbers. L&G has the higher dividend yield of 9.3 per cent, compared with 6.2 per cent for Aviva, but being close to double-digits suggests investors have questions about the sustainability of L&G’s payout, amid nervousness about its big exposure to the gilt market. Yet with a forward price-earnings ratio of 10.9, Aviva only trades at a modest premium to L&G, which is on 9.9, and at a discount to its international peers Allianz, on 11.9, and Zurich, on 14.5.  Under Blanc, who shows no signs of planning to move on, Aviva has consistently exceeded expectations, which should give investors confidence it can do the same again with its new targets. However, this is not yet reflected in its valuation. Advice Buy Why Aviva’s track record should reassure investors about its new targets

Aviva boss rules out overseas takeovers after Direct Line deal Dame Amanda Blanc says she will have ‘lost the plot’ if she took the insurer on an acquisition spree abroad  The boss of Aviva has ruled out embarking on an overseas acquisition spree, saying she will have “lost the plot” if she attempts to rebuild the FTSE 100 insurer’s former empire abroad. Dame Amanda Blanc, who took charge of the group in July 2020, spent her first few 18 months as chief executive selling off a string of non-core businesses in other countries to focus the company on its core markets of the UK, Ireland and Canada. She has since pivoted to growing the group, including by striking a £242 million deal for Probitas last year to re-enter the Lloyd’s of London insurance market after 24 years away and an ambitious £3.7 billion takeover of Direct Line that will transform Aviva into Britain’s biggest motor insurer.  Despite her dealmaking, Blanc, 57, told The Times CEO Summit on Thursday that Aviva would not revert back to becoming a sprawling group with a string of businesses overseas on her watch.  “I think that’s when CEOs start to lose the plot, when they basically go back to the future and decide that you can do things that other people, you know, couldn’t make a success out of,” she said. Shareholder frustration with Aviva’s lacklustre stock market performance had been mounting for years by the time Blanc was appointed as chief executive. She moved quickly to assuage investors by offloading eight of the insurer’s foreign businesses in countries including Italy, France, Poland, Turkey and Singapore, raising proceeds of more than £8 billion.  This disposal programme, which was completed by December 2021, allowed her to significantly increase capital returns to investors, which have amounted to £10 billion since 2020, and pay down debt.  Since then, her focus has been on expanding Aviva in less capital intensive areas such as general insurance and wealth management. Until her bid for Direct Line late last year, she had pursued smaller bolt-on acquisitions, including a £385 million deal to buy Succession Wealth. She said on Thursday: “Unless you can be number one, two or three in your market, you don’t have the right to be there. I think that we have strong positions in the UK, Ireland, Canada. That’s where we will make our success.  “Our benefit comes from diversification. If you look at the UK business, we’re either number one, two or three in every single product line. We’re the only insurer that can do health, wealth, retirement, insurance.” Aviva’s acquisition of the smaller London-listed rival Direct Line is due to formally complete early next month. Blanc said that her team “will integrate the business at pace”. Aviva shares were worth about 260p when Blanc took charge. They edged down by ½p, or 0.1 per cent, to 615¼p by the close of trading on Thursday.

Aviva boss says climate change is ‘key’ despite net-zero backlash Amanda Blanc insists that a warming planet is a crucial issue for the insurer, despite the growing sentiment against green initiatives in the financial sector  The boss of Aviva has insisted that climate change remains “critical” for the insurer, despite a wider backlash against net-zero initiatives in the financial sector and its own problems hitting green targets. Pressure on the climate agenda has mounted in recent months, particularly in the United States, where banks and asset managers have rowed back on some of their environmental commitments after the victory of Donald Trump, a climate change sceptic, in November’s presidential election. HSBC, Britain’s biggest bank, revealed last week that it was pushing back one of its main net-zero targets by two decades to 2050.  In its annual report on Thursday, Aviva, one of Britain’s biggest insurers and asset managers, also warned that it was facing obstacles in achieving its goal ofbecoming a net-zero business by 2040.  It said “the complexities and challenges are coming into sharper focus” and highlighted the problem of measuring greenhouse gas emissions from the companies in which it was an investor or provided underwriting services. “When these emissions are aggregated at a portfolio level, it introduces significant double-counting. Based on what we understand today and the low degree of control we have over these emissions, we do not currently see a route to net zero,” it said, adding that it was “committed to using our best endeavours to address them”.  Dame Amanda Blanc, the chief executive of Aviva, said that “as an insurer, climate change is critical to us” as global warming drove an increase in extreme weather events. She pointed to Canada, where Aviva has a big business and which last year suffered its worst natural catastrophe season after the country was ravaged by hailstorms, flooding and wildfires in quick succession in July and August. This has dealt a blow to insurers covering the cost of the damage and disruption. “Undeniably we would see that linked to climate change,” Blanc said of the rising frequency of extreme weather events. “We are an insurer and we have to be able to predict our pricing for us to be able to underwrite risk.” She was speaking as Aviva posted a 20 per cent jump in annual operating profits last year to almost £1.77 billion, surpassing the £1.67 billion expected by City analysts. The company was boosted by growth in general insurance premiums — an area that is drawing scrutiny from politicians because of a recent surge in the cost of car cover, which has led to claims that the industry is profiteering. Blanc, 57, pointed to the rising cost of car parts and wages in repair shops as having driven up the cost of covering claims across the market. Aviva is poised to expand its motor business through its pending £3.7 billion takeover of Direct Line, which was agreed in December and was “on track”, Blanc said.    Asked whether she was open to pursuing other acquisitions, she said “we’ve got quite a lot on our plate”, having also bought a business from AIG last year and Probitas, a Lloyd’s of London insurer. “We don’t really need to do any acquisitions, but never say never.” The deal spree caps a near five-year turnaround of Aviva by Blanc. The company said in its annual report that its board remuneration committee had handed her a 10 per cent salary increase to £1.23 million this year to reflect her “exceptional” performance. Her total pay for 2024 was £7.2 million, including an annual bonus, share awards and other benefits. That compared with £7.3 million in 2023. Aviva shares closed up 22p, or 4.2 per cent, at 546¾p, their highest in more than 15 years.

Aviva increases dividend as profits rise above £1bn Insurer’s 33,000 staff will receive £500 in free Aviva shares in recognition of the £3.7bn acquisition of Direct Line  The Aviva chief executive Dame Amanda Blanc hailed an “outstanding” first half for the insurer as operating profits were pushed past the £1 billion mark and the integration of Direct Line Group was described as “well under way”. Stand-out performances in wealth management and health insurance lifted operating profit 22 per cent to £1.07 billion, well in excess of the £972 million consensus forecast by analysts, and the interim dividend was raised 10 per cent to 13.1p. Blanc also announced a payment of £500 in free Aviva shares to each of the 33,000 employees worldwide in recognition of the £3.7 billion acquisition of Direct Line, which completed last month.  There was, however, no additional detail on the synergies expected from the acquisition of the rival motor insurer, which Aviva put at £125 million when the deal was struck in December. Blanc promised a full update in November. She also sounded a cautionary note on pension risk transfer deals, revealing that transaction volumes were down in the first half and, because of increasing competition, Aviva would be disciplined on pricing on future deals.  The profits improvement drove the Aviva share price up 16¾p, or 2.5 per cent, to close at 675¾p. Aviva boasted that as a result of the Direct Line deal it had leapfrogged both Barclays and NatWest to become the second most widely used financial-services firm in the UK, behind Lloyds Banking Group, with 21 million customers. Blanc has pushed through an extensive simplification and international retrenchment of the group since arriving in July 2020, and the successful integration of Direct Line is now seen as her biggest immediate test. The completion of the deal on July 1 was too late for it to be consolidated in the results, but Aviva said that Direct Line’s motor policy customers had fallen by 6 per cent to 3.7 million and its non-motor client base was down 4 per cent to 4.9 million. Premium revenues for the two categories were flat, however, at £1.34 billion and £500 million, respectively, and the overall net insurance margin at Direct Line increased by 7.6 percentage points to 9.4 per cent.  Analysts seized on the numbers as evidence that prices at Direct Line were now hardening. Philip Kett at Jefferies said the data “implies that prices have risen by a mid-single-digit level at a time when peers cut prices by that amount”.  Blanc said the Direct Line experience reflected Aviva’s own in general insurance, where there had been an improvement between the first and second quarters. Its general insurance premiums rose 7 per cent in the half to £6.29 billion, but she said there would still be “some areas of rate softening” in the second half. On pension risk transfer deals, Aviva reported £2 billion of sales, down from £2.3 billion last time, although in the few weeks since June 30 it landed another £1.1 billion of transactions. Aviva is fighting with Legal & General, Phoenix, M&G and Pension Insurance Corporation in the increasingly competitive business of taking over legacy pension schemes from sponsoring employers. Recently it clinched another £270 million of PRT business, also known as bulk annuity transfers, from the supermarket chain Morrisons. Blanc warned that competition for deals had increased and she would be “very disciplined” in bidding for new business, but she stuck with her target of £15 billion to £20 billion of PRT deals in 2025-27.  The wealth division, which includes running workplace pension schemes for employers, had net inflows of £5.8 billion, up 16 per cent. Health insurance reported a 14 per cent increase in premiums to more than £1 billion as more consumers opted for private cover in the face of difficulties faced by the NHS.

Aviva left my £110k pension in cash for years without telling me  Our consumer champion goes on the hunt for thousands in lost growth after the insurer silently removed a reader’s retirement pot from the market — then stopped him accessing it  Ihave a pension with the insurance company Aviva which is valued at more than £110,000. Over the years, I have taken the odd lump sum from time to time to fund various projects.  In 2023 I was looking to withdraw another lump sum so I could renovate my kitchen and bathroom, but I was shocked to learn that the fund was no longer invested and my money had been sitting in cash since December 2021 when I turned 75. Aviva had not warned me about this and the first I heard of it was when it wrote to me two years later. I was also surprised when Aviva said I could no longer withdraw lump sums. This pension was my main source of capital so I cannot carry out my renovations until I have access to this pot again.  I asked Aviva what my options were, but it said I must complete a form before it could give me any information. Frankly, I did not understand the form so I decided to take financial advice from a company called Fifteen Financial Planning, which is part of St James’s Place. I have always been clear that my preference is for drawdown so that I can take lump sums when I want. But my financial adviser said there was nothing I could do besides buying an annuity to give me a regular income.  He then spent some time getting quotes from various pension providers and eventually proposed that I buy an annuity with Legal & General. I was frustrated because all I wanted to do was withdraw some money, but seeing no other option I reluctantly agreed to this and signed the necessary forms. That was last July, but my pension is still with Aviva.  My adviser spoke to L&G which said that Aviva has refused to transfer the funds. I don’t understand how Aviva can sit on my pension pot of more than £110,000 and leave it in cash for years without contacting me. I have been given no explanation as to why it did not contact me as I approached 75, and I also don’t understand why it is now refusing to transfer my pension. Keith, Lincolnshire Katherine Denham writes I was appalled that Aviva had given you no warning about what would happen to your pension once you turned 75. Even worse was that, unknown to you, your nest egg had been missing out on investment returns for years and now you were struggling to access it.  In the 12 months before you reached 75, Aviva should have sent you three letters reminding you to make a decision about your pension. But it told me that it confused this pension with another policy that you hold with the company, which is why you never received any reminders. Getting two policies mixed up is a momentous mistake and I was surprised that Aviva hadn’t pulled out all the stops to put things right.  All you wanted to do was to make ad-hoc withdrawals, but I’m afraid there was a problem with this: your pension was set up so that once you reached 75, you could use the money only to buy an annuity. This is common with older policies that were set up before the new pension freedom rules were introduced in 2015. But wasn’t it possible to switch to another scheme so that you were no longer bound by these rules? According to Fifteen, Aviva said an annuity was your only option, which is why your financial adviser steered you in that direction. Aviva said it had believed that was the case based on the information it had last year, but that it needed your adviser to complete a form to establish whether you had any other options, including transferring out. Yet there seemed to be some miscommunication over this paperwork.  Aviva said it had reminded your adviser that it needed this form, but he told me that it wasn’t until December that the company made this clear, five months after he had initiated the transfer to L&G. Your adviser said Fifteen had chased Aviva “religiously” since August and “at no point did Aviva tell us that this paperwork was required”. When Aviva sent the form in December, it had the wrong client’s name on it, which it blamed on a typing error. It wasn’t until February that Aviva gave your adviser the information he needed to complete the form. At that point you could have switched to L&G ,but you were thoroughly against the idea of buying an annuity, so the transfer was cancelled. I was pleased that Fifteen did not charge you any advice fees. Given the issues you had with Aviva, I hoped that it would make an exception in your case and come up with a solution that you were happy with. But even after my involvement you were given mixed messages about what was feasible. You were becoming more frustrated by the minute, so I asked Aviva what was going on. Eventually it agreed to let you set up a self-invested personal pension (Sipp) so you can withdraw payments as and when you like, which you were satisfied with.  Aviva said: “We are sorry that we did not contact our customer prior to his 75th birthday. We apologise for this error and the subsequent delays it caused. We have reviewed our processes to help ensure this doesn’t happen again.” It paid you £18,056 so that you’re not financially disadvantaged by the time that your pension was left in cash. This interest has been added to your pension of £110,542, giving you a total of £128,598, which has been transferred to your Sipp ready to be invested.  Aviva also gave you £500 compensation. Given that your ordeal had been going on for so long and caused you a huge amount of stress, neither you nor I were impressed with that figure, but it wouldn’t offer any more.  You said: “There was no end in sight until you intervened, so I want to say a very big thank you for getting in touch with Aviva on my behalf.”

Aviva steers more pension savings into private markets New default option will allocate an initial 25 per cent of an employee’s pension pot to private equity, infrastructure and property  Millions of British pension savers could have up to 25 per cent of their pots handed to private market groups including KKR and Apollo Global to manage, as Aviva pushes ahead with plans to shift more client money into unlisted assets. Aviva, a big player in managing UK employers’ pension schemes, said it was launching a new “default” option for employers and employees that would produce far greater exposure to private assets. The new “My Future Vision” (MFV) option will allocate an initial 25 per cent of an employee’s pension pot to private equity, private credit, infrastructure and property. That compares to a 10 per cent allocation under its existing “My Future Focus” (MFF) default and 0 per cent under its low-cost “My Future” default.  Employers and employees will still be able to opt out, but in practice few tend to. Defaults are powerful “nudge” tools in determining the choices made by clients. Aviva expects the new default to lead to 5-6 per cent of people’s pots going into specifically UK-based private equity and infrastructure, which would align with the company’s recent promise under the Mansion House accord agreed with Rachel Reeves.  Aviva has assets of £419 billion and is one of Britain’s biggest managers of defined contribution (DC) pension schemes, including £15 billion for 800,000 people in its auto-enrolment master trust. Initially, the new default will be offered only to the £30 billion of trust-based schemes it manages but Mai Rajah, Aviva’s director of investments, said he hoped the same approach could one day be applied to contract-based schemes, when the rules allow it.  Members would benefit from higher risk-adjusted returns, he added. The trade-off because of a lack of liquidity and higher fees in private assets was “worthwhile”.  The new default, Rajah said, was “a major step forward for DC pension schemes when it comes to the scope and sophistication of private market investing”.  The shift to private assets is in full swing. Legal & General and Fidelity have also made moves to put more DC pension fund assets into the asset class, which has produced strong returns in the past few decades and improved diversification. As well as KKR and Apollo, Aviva has signed up Neuberger Berman, Stepstone and Invesco to manage the money. All five are headquartered in the US.  The government has argued that UK savers are missing out on higher eventual pensions because many are almost entirely invested in listed shares and bonds.  However, some experts are more cautious, arguing that the strong returns from private assets in the past may not be repeated in future, that the asset class is less liquid and less transparent and that savers will also have to shoulder much higher fees. Private credit in particular is an untested asset class, ballooning since 2008 when crisis-hit banks pulled back from much corporate lending.  The Mansion House pledge of many fund managers to allocate 5 per cent of pots to UK private assets has raised eyebrows with some, who argue ministers should not be pressuring the British public to take more potential risk when their own defined-benefit pensions are guaranteed in all circumstances. The asset allocation shift could also be seen as a fresh blow to listed markets, including the London Stock Exchange. While Aviva’s existing MFF default puts 90 per cent of a young earner’s contributions into listed shares, the new MFV default will allocate only 75 per cent.  Contract-based pension schemes are for now precluded from easily investing in private assets because they are bound by a fees cap of 0.75 per cent. For trust-based schemes, the cap excludes private assets. Neuberger and Stepstone will manage the private equity allocation, KKR will be in charge of infrastructure investment, Apollo and Stepstone will handle private credit and Invesco will manage the real estate assets. The in-house team at Aviva Investors will also be given some mandates.  The private markets allocation in the new default will start at 25 per cent for young employees and drop to 20 per cent by retirement age. Within the overall private markets bucket, 35 per cent will be allocated to private equity, 25 per cent each to private credit and infrastructure and 15 per cent to real estate. Asset class allocation, rather than the choice of individual stocks, has been much more important over the years in determining pot sizes and therefore retirement incomes.

Aviva wins backing for plan to cancel ‘irredeemable’ shares Investors have been offered a premium to resolve the issue after an abortive attempt seven years ago  Investors have approved a plan by Aviva to cancel £450 million of supposedly “irredeemable” preference shares seven years after a City backlash forced the FTSE 100 insurer to abandon a previous attempt. Aviva provoked a furore in 2018 when it revealed it was looking at repurchasing the securities at par value, which would have resulted in big losses for investors because the shares were trading above that level.  The debacle contributed to the ousting of the company’s chief executive Mark Wilson later that year and resulted in the insurer subsequently being publicly censured by the Financial Conduct Authority over its handling of the affair.  Yet the issue of cancelling the preference shares, which were first issued in 1992 and 1993, has not gone away. Rule changes mean that from the beginning of next year, the securities will no longer count towards Aviva’s regulatory capital. The shares also cost Aviva about £38 million a year in coupon payments to investors. The insurer revived its plan to cancel them last month but, unlike last time, it is offering investors a premium to the shares’ market value.   Aviva also pledged not to cancel the securities without securing the specific approval of at least 75 per cent of voting preference shareholders, which it obtained on Tuesday during four investor meetings held to decide on the issue. While both ordinary and preference shareholders overwhelmingly backed Aviva’s plan at different votes, it was not without criticism.  Philip Meadowcroft, a holder of ordinary Aviva shares, told George Culmer, the insurer’s chairman, that “you have thrown irreedemability under the proverbial bus by replacing it with words like ‘cancelling’ and ‘retiring’.” He added: “Irredeemable, chairman, has to mean unchangeable — nothing more, nothing less.”  Culmer, who was not at Aviva during the attempt to tackle the issue in 2018, insisted the insurer was “not doing this in a perfunctory, unfair manner” and that it had always had the ability to cancel the securities.  “Yes, they were marketed as irredeemable, but yes, the world has moved on,” he said. Aviva’s ordinary shares were up 10¾p, or 2.1 per cent, at 522½p by the close on Tuesday.

My wife has months to live but Aviva rejected our £20k life insurance claim Our consumer champion Katherine Denham helps a couple caught in a wrangle over a terminal illness payout Katherine Denham , Consumer champion Sunday May 18 2025, 12.00am, The Sunday Times  In 2019 my wife was diagnosed with terminal lung cancer and began taking medication to prevent it from spreading. Sadly this medication stopped working last year and the cancer has spread to her lymph nodes, spine, liver and breast. She had emergency surgery in October to remove a large tumour in her bowel and is about to start chemotherapy to try to slow down the progression of the cancer. We have a decreasing life insurance policy with the insurer Aviva and have been paying £20.46 a month since 2011. The policy also gives us a payout if either of us are diagnosed with a terminal illness. We first claimed in 2019 but Aviva rejected our claim because her doctor said she was likely to survive for more than 12 months. When it became clear that my wife’s medication was no longer working, we claimed again last September. We had a report from her oncologist saying that her life expectancy could be less than 12 months. We were shocked when Aviva finally responded in December to once again reject our claim. We appealed against this decision and Aviva apologised for the delay but said it was very busy due to an unprecedented level of demand on its resources.  We have complained to Aviva but it has been four months and we haven’t heard back. Our policy should give us about £20,000, which we would have used to have a more comfortable life. We also hoped to use some of the money to book a special family holiday which we could have cherished with our three daughters and two grandchildren. As my wife is now so unwell, I don’t think that’s possible any more.   We are struggling with her condition enough as it is, so this problem with Aviva just brings extra stress and pressure when we least need it. We just feel Aviva has been far too slow, especially considering my wife has just months left to live. Name and address supplied Katherine Denham writes  I was deeply saddened to hear of your wife’s illness and that you had been living with such uncertainty for so many years. It was completely understandable that you wanted the assurance of a payout to make things easier in her final months. Like many life insurance policies, yours offers an early payout before death if you’re diagnosed with a terminal illness and given less than 12 months to live. But there was another time limit to consider. Within 18 months of your policy’s expiration date, you can no longer claim for terminal illness, meaning that Aviva would only pay out on death. Your policy was due to expire in March next year, but thankfully you had raised your claim just before the 18-month window and so it was still valid. Considering that time was of the essence, it was incredibly disappointing that you hadn’t heard from Aviva for months. It told me that after you appealed its decision in December, it got another oncologist to review the medical information and look at the average life expectancy of others undergoing the same treatment.  That oncologist thought it was plausible that your wife could live for more than a year, although this depended on how she responded to treatment. Aviva should have sent you a letter explaining this but it failed to do so, which it blamed on human error. After I called the company it reviewed your case again. As it became clear that your wife’s treatment wasn’t working, it decided to pay out your claim based on its value in September, giving you £23,455. Aviva said: “We were very sorry to hear of our customer’s diagnosis and we understand the seriousness of her condition. We did review this again following the customer’s appeal in December but we regret that we have no record that our response was sent, for which we are extremely sorry. “With the new information recently provided that the customer is sadly no longer responding to treatment, we now have a clearer picture of her life expectancy. As this is now likely to be less than 12 months, we believe it is appropriate in these circumstances to pay out the remaining value of the policy.” It has given you a £500 goodwill payment and you said: “I cannot thank you enough, we are so grateful.”

Pension funds could be forced to invest in UK projects The government is set to give itself the power to force investment in areas it regards as important if funds fail to comply with voluntary pact  The government is set to give itself the power to mandate pension funds to invest cash in UK projects if they fail to comply with a voluntary pact due to be announced this week.  A review, led by pensions minister Torsten Bell, is expected to recommend that the government is given the option to mandate pension fund investment, according to a source close to the government. The review will be published later this month after an agreement this week by more than 15 major pension fund investors — thought to include FTSE 100 giants L&G, Aviva, Phoenix and M&G — to invest up to 10 per cent of their assets in fast-growing companies and infrastructure, with half of that designated for the UK, by 2030.  Known as the Mansion House Accord, it will amount to at least £25 billion of extra investment from workplace pension funds. But it has exposed divisions in the City about whether forcing pension funds to invest in certain areas clashes with their “fiduciary duty”, which is to provide the best return to their investors. There has been speculation that the government will use a regulatory “backstop” to force investment in areas it regards as important.  One senior City source warned there was “growing concern” that it would lead to lower returns for pension funds.  In addition, a push to invest more in unlisted assets could be more expensive for pension fund holders. Fees charged by pension fund providers to invest in private markets are higher than those for investing in the stock market. The Accord is intended to build on the 2023 Mansion House Compact, under which pension funds agreed to invest 5 per cent of their assets in unlisted equities by 2030. It is being orchestrated by Alastair King, the lord mayor, a figurehead position at the City of London Corporation — the local authority for the financial district. A wide range of pension fund providers are said to have signed up to the Accord but it is not clear whether Scottish Widows, owned by Lloyds Banking Group, has done so. Scottish Widows is the biggest of the default fund providers for workplace pensions.  The Treasury and the City of London Corporation declined to comment.

Risks of private markets deserve public scrutiny If Rachel Reeves wants pension pots to seek higher returns from sometimes opaque investments, the Treasury needs to take some interest in overseeing them Alistair Osborne Friday January 09 2026, 12.01am, The Times As fans of private markets go, few beat Rachel Reeves. She has so much faith in private equity, private credit, private real estate and other private stuff — venture capital, infrastructure funds and, as she put it, “exciting start-ups” — that she coerced 17 pension providers into the Mansion House Accord.  What that? A so-called “voluntary” agreement signed last May by the likes of Aviva, Legal & General and M&G to allocate “at least 10 per cent” of their default defined contribution pension funds into private assets by 2030, with 5 per cent of that going into the UK. In short, tens of billions of quid from our pensions pots. Still, what better than that for “delivering growth” and “giving working people greater security in retirement”, as the chancellor said at the time? And who knows, maybe Reeves will be proved right. Private markets have generally outperformed the public sort since the 2008 financial crisis. Yet, they also bring all sorts of risks, as a report headed “Private markets: unknown unknowns” from the House of Lords financial services regulation committee spelt out on Thursday. And the least you’d expect, given Reeves’s enthusiasm, is that the Treasury is fully across those risks. • Treasury ‘doesn’t appreciate’ potential risks of private markets boom Instead, here is what the committee, chaired by Lord Forsyth of Drumlean, found: “HM Treasury’s evidence demonstrated a limited grasp of the concerns raised during this inquiry, which suggested passivity in the face of potential risks to the UK’s financial stability arising from the growth of private markets.”  How reassuring is that? As the report relates, since 2008 these markets have taken off, with global assets under management quadrupling to $16 trillion — three-quarters in the US but with Britain in second place, presiding over $185 billion in the first quarter of 2025. The key reasons for the growth? Quantitative easing and low interest rates, which prompted investors to seek racier returns in private equity and private credit.  The problem? As it says on the tin, such markets are private, so it’s tricky to know precisely what’s going on — even if regulators are paying increasing attention. The lordships say there is “insufficient data” to conclude whether there’s systemic risk. But the Bank of England’s Financial Policy Committee identified three key features last July that could send things off in that direction: “The widespread use of leverage, the opacity of valuations and extent of reliance on rating agencies, and the correlation with other risky funding markets.”  In the private credit market, last September’s collapse of two US companies — car parts supplier First Brands and sub-prime auto lender Tricolor — ramped up fears over weak lending criteria. As the Bank of England governor, Andrew Bailey, asked at the time: “Are these cases idiosyncratic or are they what I call the canary in the coal mine?” As the report notes, too, we already have examples of the “interconnectedness” between private markets and the wider financial system: 2021’s collapse of hedge fund Archegos Capital Management, say, which caused $10 billion of losses to global banks.  Quantifying such risks in a world where the likes of private equity continuation funds meet AI-fuelled stock markets is tricky — not least when you add Trumpian political risk. But a Lords committee shouldn’t need to spell out to the Treasury that, with the taxpayer on the hook for any blow-up, it must take more “responsibility” for overseeing private markets rather than simply “delegating” it to regulators. Not least when Reeves wants more private stuff in our pension funds.

Tax on salary sacrifice ‘bad news’ for Britain Aviva chief urges Rachel Reeves to think again about capping the schemes in her budget, saying it would be bad for Britain  A tax raid on salary sacrifice schemes would be “bad news” for Britain because it would penalise employers and disincentive workers from saving for retirement, the boss of one of the country’s biggest pension providers has warned. Dame Amanda Blanc, the chief executive of Aviva, said on Thursday that “we would urge the chancellor to think very carefully about changes to salary sacrifice” in the forthcoming budget. Under the schemes, employees give up part of their pay in return for pension contributions and other benefits. The money comes out of wages before it is taxed and, by reducing salaries, means workers and employers pay less national insurance.  Currently, workers can put as much as £60,000 a year tax-free into pensions.  However, tax breaks for salary sacrifice are set to be curtailed by Rachel Reeves, with the chancellor expected to use her budget on November 26 to announce a cap of £2,000 a year on the amount that can be sacrificed without being subject to national insurance, which could raise £2 billion a year.  This would push up national insurance costs for employees and employers and has prompted warnings from pensions experts that companies are likely to respond by making less generous pension contributions. “What you’re effectively doing is penalising those employers that actually contribute more to employees’ pensions,” Blanc said. “But you’re also saying to people who save for their pension that perhaps they shouldn’t do it, and I think that’s bad news long-term for the UK if you think about the fact that 15 million people in the UK are not saving enough.”  It would represent another national insurance hit for companies, after Reeves used her first budget in October last year to increase employer contributions, which angered many bosses. Blanc warned: “The actual cost to employers of removing that NI benefit from salary sacrifice is not going to be insignificant.”  She was speaking as Aviva posted third-quarter results, in which the FTSE 100 insurance and savings group almost doubled the cost savings it expects to realise from its recent £3.7 billion takeover of the insurer Direct Line. It now expects to deliver £225 million of run-rate savings by 2028 from the deal, up from its previous forecast of £125 million. Aviva also said it expected to generate an operating profit of £2.2 billion this year before any contribution from Direct Line, meaning it will have reached its target of £2 billion by 2026, a year earlier than planned, and set new three-year goals for the business which include delivering a return on equity of more than 20 per cent by 2028.  Analysts at Bank of America reiterated their “buy” advice on Aviva’s stock to clients and told them: “We think it will beat its new targets.” However, Aviva shares slid 42½p, or 6.1 per cent, to close at 650p, having surged by about 40 per cent since the start of the year, with many of the target upgrades announced by the insurer on Thursday already anticipated by investors.

UK’s biggest pension funds back regional growth drive Legal & General and the Universities Superannuation Scheme are among 20 members of a new investment partnership called Sterling 20  Affordable housing and rural broadband provision will be the first beneficiaries of a new regional investment drive by 20 of Britain’s biggest pension providers and insurers. Legal & General has made a £2 billion commitment to invest in delivering 10,000 affordable homes by 2030, while Nest is to invest £40 million in fibre broadband in Scotland and northern England, and provide £100 million to Schroders Capital to make UK investments.  • The latest business and markets news — follow live The companies are part of a new partnership called Sterling 20 that will be established at a first regional investment summit with Rachel Reeves in Birmingham this week.  The Treasury said the initiative was designed to “channel the nation’s savings into key infrastructure and fast-growing businesses in key modern industrial strategy sectors like AI and fintech”.  The 20 participants also include M&G, Phoenix, Aviva, Royal London and the Universities Superannuation Scheme.  The government is trying to encourage British pension funds to invest more in the UK and especially in growth industries and private businesses. “Even a small shift towards investing in UK infrastructure would unlock billions,” the Treasury said.  In May, the government co-ordinated the Mansion House accord in which 17 pension providers committed to invest at least 5 per cent of their main default funds in UK private markets. These 17 providers have all joined Sterling 20, alongside Rothesay and PIC, the annuity providers, and the Pension Protection Fund.  In the summer the government revamped the Office for Investment — a joint unit of the Treasury, business department and No 10 — to help encourage investment in Britain.  The Treasury said that the office’s “unique position as a bridge between government, investors and local leaders will allow it to match transformational investment opportunities with capital”. António Simões, chief executive of Legal & General, said: “Our £2 billion commitment, targeted at housing, infrastructure, and urban regeneration, will help unlock the investment needed in productive assets across the country — creating jobs, strengthening communities, and driving both regional and national growth.”  Ian Cornelius, chief executive of Nest, said the UK had “exceptional investment opportunities” and was “a cornerstone of our strategy”. He added: “We have already committed around £4 billion to UK private markets, and by 2030 we expect this to rise to around £12 billion. A strong pipeline of opportunities will be essential to realising this growth for the benefit of our members and the UK economy.” As part of the summit, the US infrastructure company Welltower will announce plans to more than triple investment in the UK to $12 billion over the next five to ten years.  An S&P 500 listed long-term investor specialising in the healthcare infrastructure sector, Welltower has already invested $4 billion in the UK over the last decade. It operates 299 social care properties across the UK, from Dorset to Scotland and supports a supply chain employing almost 28,000 people.  The company’s existing plans will add more than 2,500 extra beds to social care capacity, with 90 per cent of these outside of London. It comes amid a severe supply and demand imbalance for social care in Britain, which ministers are seeking to address.

articles.article_id: Are there good reasons to invest in Aviva
articles.article_filename: Are there good reasons to invest in Aviva.docx
articles.body: Are there good reasons to invest in Aviva? Chief executive Dame Amanda Blanc says the outlook for the insurer has never been better. Do the numbers back up her confidence?  Dame Amanda Blanc declared that “the outlook for Aviva has never been better” last week when she reported third-quarter figures and set out new three-year targets for the FTSE 100 insurer. Is she right and if so, is this optimism reflected in the company’s valuation? Thrust into the top job in July 2020 after a short stint as a non-executive on its board, the insurance industry veteran has already spearheaded a turnaround at Aviva that has earned plaudits in the City.  Aviva was at a low ebb when she took charge, with investors frustrated by its lacklustre stock market returns under a succession of bosses. Blanc quickly took action to address these concerns by selling a string of non-core businesses overseas, including Aviva’s operations in France, Italy, Turkey, Poland, Vietnam and Singapore, a reshaping of the group that generated proceeds of more than £8 billion.  This not only streamlined the insurer by refocusing it on its core markets of the UK, Ireland and Canada, it enabled Blanc to step up capital returns to shareholders, with more than £10 billion handed back over the past five years.  It was a pivot praised by Cevian, an activist investor which revealed in 2021 that it was a big shareholder in Aviva and sold out in 2023, when it praised Aviva for having been “transformed from a poorly performing conglomerate to a focused and well-performing insurance company”.  However, despite Blanc’s programme of disposals and capital returns, it has not simply been a strategy of retrenchment. There have been acquisitions too, including a £385 million deal in 2022 to buy Succession Wealth to expand Aviva’s presence in the wealth management market.  The following year Aviva spent £460 million on AIG’s UK protection business and £100 million on Optiom in Canada, while in March 2024 the company announced it was re-entering the Lloyd’s of London insurance market after 24 years through a £242 million deal for Probitas. All of these deals enlarged Aviva’s capital-light operations, helping it to differentiate it from its more capital-heavy rival Legal & General. Then came Blanc’s biggest gamble yet, with Aviva’s £3.7 billion agreement last December to acquire troubled rival Direct Line. A further expansion of its capital-light business, the takeover, which was finalised in July this year, makes Aviva Britain’s biggest motor insurer.  Aviva had initially estimated it would save £125 million a year by eliminating duplicated roles, digitising Direct Line’s operations and making other cost cuts following the takeover. As was widely expected, however, it significantly increased its cost synergy estimate to £225 million in its trading update last week, when it also said it expected at least £500 million of capital synergies from the deal.   There was other good news. Blanc had been aiming to deliver £2 billion of operating profit in 2026 but Aviva said it would hit this target a year early, with the group expecting to generate £2.2 billion in 2025. Even stripping out the contribution made by Direct Line this year, Aviva’s operating profits are still forecast to surpass £2 billion.  The insurer also set new goals for 2028, saying it was targeting a return on equity of more than 20 per cent and aiming to grow adjusted earnings at an average annual rate of 11 per cent over the next three years.  The turnaround Blanc has already achieved has powered Aviva’s share price higher. Its stock closed on Wednesday evening at 632p, up 134 per cent since she was appointed, although it has dipped in recent days amid some disappointment with its third-quarter numbers. L&G has the higher dividend yield of 9.3 per cent, compared with 6.2 per cent for Aviva, but being close to double-digits suggests investors have questions about the sustainability of L&G’s payout, amid nervousness about its big exposure to the gilt market. Yet with a forward price-earnings ratio of 10.9, Aviva only trades at a modest premium to L&G, which is on 9.9, and at a discount to its international peers Allianz, on 11.9, and Zurich, on 14.5.  Under Blanc, who shows no signs of planning to move on, Aviva has consistently exceeded expectations, which should give investors confidence it can do the same again with its new targets. However, this is not yet reflected in its valuation. Advice Buy Why Aviva’s track record should reassure investors about its new targets

articles.article_id: Aviva boss rules out overseas takeovers after Direct Line deal
articles.article_filename: Aviva boss rules out overseas takeovers after Direct Line deal.docx
articles.body: Aviva boss rules out overseas takeovers after Direct Line deal Dame Amanda Blanc says she will have ‘lost the plot’ if she took the insurer on an acquisition spree abroad  The boss of Aviva has ruled out embarking on an overseas acquisition spree, saying she will have “lost the plot” if she attempts to rebuild the FTSE 100 insurer’s former empire abroad. Dame Amanda Blanc, who took charge of the group in July 2020, spent her first few 18 months as chief executive selling off a string of non-core businesses in other countries to focus the company on its core markets of the UK, Ireland and Canada. She has since pivoted to growing the group, including by striking a £242 million deal for Probitas last year to re-enter the Lloyd’s of London insurance market after 24 years away and an ambitious £3.7 billion takeover of Direct Line that will transform Aviva into Britain’s biggest motor insurer.  Despite her dealmaking, Blanc, 57, told The Times CEO Summit on Thursday that Aviva would not revert back to becoming a sprawling group with a string of businesses overseas on her watch.  “I think that’s when CEOs start to lose the plot, when they basically go back to the future and decide that you can do things that other people, you know, couldn’t make a success out of,” she said. Shareholder frustration with Aviva’s lacklustre stock market performance had been mounting for years by the time Blanc was appointed as chief executive. She moved quickly to assuage investors by offloading eight of the insurer’s foreign businesses in countries including Italy, France, Poland, Turkey and Singapore, raising proceeds of more than £8 billion.  This disposal programme, which was completed by December 2021, allowed her to significantly increase capital returns to investors, which have amounted to £10 billion since 2020, and pay down debt.  Since then, her focus has been on expanding Aviva in less capital intensive areas such as general insurance and wealth management. Until her bid for Direct Line late last year, she had pursued smaller bolt-on acquisitions, including a £385 million deal to buy Succession Wealth. She said on Thursday: “Unless you can be number one, two or three in your market, you don’t have the right to be there. I think that we have strong positions in the UK, Ireland, Canada. That’s where we will make our success.  “Our benefit comes from diversification. If you look at the UK business, we’re either number one, two or three in every single product line. We’re the only insurer that can do health, wealth, retirement, insurance.” Aviva’s acquisition of the smaller London-listed rival Direct Line is due to formally complete early next month. Blanc said that her team “will integrate the business at pace”. Aviva shares were worth about 260p when Blanc took charge. They edged down by ½p, or 0.1 per cent, to 615¼p by the close of trading on Thursday.

articles.article_id: Aviva boss says climate change is
articles.article_filename: Aviva boss says climate change is.docx
articles.body: Aviva boss says climate change is ‘key’ despite net-zero backlash Amanda Blanc insists that a warming planet is a crucial issue for the insurer, despite the growing sentiment against green initiatives in the financial sector  The boss of Aviva has insisted that climate change remains “critical” for the insurer, despite a wider backlash against net-zero initiatives in the financial sector and its own problems hitting green targets. Pressure on the climate agenda has mounted in recent months, particularly in the United States, where banks and asset managers have rowed back on some of their environmental commitments after the victory of Donald Trump, a climate change sceptic, in November’s presidential election. HSBC, Britain’s biggest bank, revealed last week that it was pushing back one of its main net-zero targets by two decades to 2050.  In its annual report on Thursday, Aviva, one of Britain’s biggest insurers and asset managers, also warned that it was facing obstacles in achieving its goal ofbecoming a net-zero business by 2040.  It said “the complexities and challenges are coming into sharper focus” and highlighted the problem of measuring greenhouse gas emissions from the companies in which it was an investor or provided underwriting services. “When these emissions are aggregated at a portfolio level, it introduces significant double-counting. Based on what we understand today and the low degree of control we have over these emissions, we do not currently see a route to net zero,” it said, adding that it was “committed to using our best endeavours to address them”.  Dame Amanda Blanc, the chief executive of Aviva, said that “as an insurer, climate change is critical to us” as global warming drove an increase in extreme weather events. She pointed to Canada, where Aviva has a big business and which last year suffered its worst natural catastrophe season after the country was ravaged by hailstorms, flooding and wildfires in quick succession in July and August. This has dealt a blow to insurers covering the cost of the damage and disruption. “Undeniably we would see that linked to climate change,” Blanc said of the rising frequency of extreme weather events. “We are an insurer and we have to be able to predict our pricing for us to be able to underwrite risk.” She was speaking as Aviva posted a 20 per cent jump in annual operating profits last year to almost £1.77 billion, surpassing the £1.67 billion expected by City analysts. The company was boosted by growth in general insurance premiums — an area that is drawing scrutiny from politicians because of a recent surge in the cost of car cover, which has led to claims that the industry is profiteering. Blanc, 57, pointed to the rising cost of car parts and wages in repair shops as having driven up the cost of covering claims across the market. Aviva is poised to expand its motor business through its pending £3.7 billion takeover of Direct Line, which was agreed in December and was “on track”, Blanc said.    Asked whether she was open to pursuing other acquisitions, she said “we’ve got quite a lot on our plate”, having also bought a business from AIG last year and Probitas, a Lloyd’s of London insurer. “We don’t really need to do any acquisitions, but never say never.” The deal spree caps a near five-year turnaround of Aviva by Blanc. The company said in its annual report that its board remuneration committee had handed her a 10 per cent salary increase to £1.23 million this year to reflect her “exceptional” performance. Her total pay for 2024 was £7.2 million, including an annual bonus, share awards and other benefits. That compared with £7.3 million in 2023. Aviva shares closed up 22p, or 4.2 per cent, at 546¾p, their highest in more than 15 years.

articles.article_id: Aviva increases dividend as profits rise above
articles.article_filename: Aviva increases dividend as profits rise above.docx
articles.body: Aviva increases dividend as profits rise above £1bn Insurer’s 33,000 staff will receive £500 in free Aviva shares in recognition of the £3.7bn acquisition of Direct Line  The Aviva chief executive Dame Amanda Blanc hailed an “outstanding” first half for the insurer as operating profits were pushed past the £1 billion mark and the integration of Direct Line Group was described as “well under way”. Stand-out performances in wealth management and health insurance lifted operating profit 22 per cent to £1.07 billion, well in excess of the £972 million consensus forecast by analysts, and the interim dividend was raised 10 per cent to 13.1p. Blanc also announced a payment of £500 in free Aviva shares to each of the 33,000 employees worldwide in recognition of the £3.7 billion acquisition of Direct Line, which completed last month.  There was, however, no additional detail on the synergies expected from the acquisition of the rival motor insurer, which Aviva put at £125 million when the deal was struck in December. Blanc promised a full update in November. She also sounded a cautionary note on pension risk transfer deals, revealing that transaction volumes were down in the first half and, because of increasing competition, Aviva would be disciplined on pricing on future deals.  The profits improvement drove the Aviva share price up 16¾p, or 2.5 per cent, to close at 675¾p. Aviva boasted that as a result of the Direct Line deal it had leapfrogged both Barclays and NatWest to become the second most widely used financial-services firm in the UK, behind Lloyds Banking Group, with 21 million customers. Blanc has pushed through an extensive simplification and international retrenchment of the group since arriving in July 2020, and the successful integration of Direct Line is now seen as her biggest immediate test. The completion of the deal on July 1 was too late for it to be consolidated in the results, but Aviva said that Direct Line’s motor policy customers had fallen by 6 per cent to 3.7 million and its non-motor client base was down 4 per cent to 4.9 million. Premium revenues for the two categories were flat, however, at £1.34 billion and £500 million, respectively, and the overall net insurance margin at Direct Line increased by 7.6 percentage points to 9.4 per cent.  Analysts seized on the numbers as evidence that prices at Direct Line were now hardening. Philip Kett at Jefferies said the data “implies that prices have risen by a mid-single-digit level at a time when peers cut prices by that amount”.  Blanc said the Direct Line experience reflected Aviva’s own in general insurance, where there had been an improvement between the first and second quarters. Its general insurance premiums rose 7 per cent in the half to £6.29 billion, but she said there would still be “some areas of rate softening” in the second half. On pension risk transfer deals, Aviva reported £2 billion of sales, down from £2.3 billion last time, although in the few weeks since June 30 it landed another £1.1 billion of transactions. Aviva is fighting with Legal & General, Phoenix, M&G and Pension Insurance Corporation in the increasingly competitive business of taking over legacy pension schemes from sponsoring employers. Recently it clinched another £270 million of PRT business, also known as bulk annuity transfers, from the supermarket chain Morrisons. Blanc warned that competition for deals had increased and she would be “very disciplined” in bidding for new business, but she stuck with her target of £15 billion to £20 billion of PRT deals in 2025-27.  The wealth division, which includes running workplace pension schemes for employers, had net inflows of £5.8 billion, up 16 per cent. Health insurance reported a 14 per cent increase in premiums to more than £1 billion as more consumers opted for private cover in the face of difficulties faced by the NHS.

Aviva left my £110k pension in cash for years without telling me  Our consumer champion goes on the hunt for thousands in lost growth after the insurer silently removed a reader’s retirement pot from the market — then stopped him accessing it  Ihave a pension with the insurance company Aviva which is valued at more than £110,000. Over the years, I have taken the odd lump sum from time to time to fund various projects.  In 2023 I was looking to withdraw another lump sum so I could renovate my kitchen and bathroom, but I was shocked to learn that the fund was no longer invested and my money had been sitting in cash since December 2021 when I turned 75. Aviva had not warned me about this and the first I heard of it was when it wrote to me two years later. I was also surprised when Aviva said I could no longer withdraw lump sums. This pension was my main source of capital so I cannot carry out my renovations until I have access to this pot again.  I asked Aviva what my options were, but it said I must complete a form before it could give me any information. Frankly, I did not understand the form so I decided to take financial advice from a company called Fifteen Financial Planning, which is part of St James’s Place. I have always been clear that my preference is for drawdown so that I can take lump sums when I want. But my financial adviser said there was nothing I could do besides buying an annuity to give me a regular income.  He then spent some time getting quotes from various pension providers and eventually proposed that I buy an annuity with Legal & General. I was frustrated because all I wanted to do was withdraw some money, but seeing no other option I reluctantly agreed to this and signed the necessary forms. That was last July, but my pension is still with Aviva.  My adviser spoke to L&G which said that Aviva has refused to transfer the funds. I don’t understand how Aviva can sit on my pension pot of more than £110,000 and leave it in cash for years without contacting me. I have been given no explanation as to why it did not contact me as I approached 75, and I also don’t understand why it is now refusing to transfer my pension. Keith, Lincolnshire Katherine Denham writes I was appalled that Aviva had given you no warning about what would happen to your pension once you turned 75. Even worse was that, unknown to you, your nest egg had been missing out on investment returns for years and now you were struggling to access it.  In the 12 months before you reached 75, Aviva should have sent you three letters reminding you to make a decision about your pension. But it told me that it confused this pension with another policy that you hold with the company, which is why you never received any reminders. Getting two policies mixed up is a momentous mistake and I was surprised that Aviva hadn’t pulled out all the stops to put things right.  All you wanted to do was to make ad-hoc withdrawals, but I’m afraid there was a problem with this: your pension was set up so that once you reached 75, you could use the money only to buy an annuity. This is common with older policies that were set up before the new pension freedom rules were introduced in 2015. But wasn’t it possible to switch to another scheme so that you were no longer bound by these rules? According to Fifteen, Aviva said an annuity was your only option, which is why your financial adviser steered you in that direction. Aviva said it had believed that was the case based on the information it had last year, but that it needed your adviser to complete a form to establish whether you had any other options, including transferring out. Yet there seemed to be some miscommunication over this paperwork.  Aviva said it had reminded your adviser that it needed this form, but he told me that it wasn’t until December that the company made this clear, five months after he had initiated the transfer to L&G. Your adviser said Fifteen had chased Aviva “religiously” since August and “at no point did Aviva tell us that this paperwork was required”. When Aviva sent the form in December, it had the wrong client’s name on it, which it blamed on a typing error. It wasn’t until February that Aviva gave your adviser the information he needed to complete the form. At that point you could have switched to L&G ,but you were thoroughly against the idea of buying an annuity, so the transfer was cancelled. I was pleased that Fifteen did not charge you any advice fees. Given the issues you had with Aviva, I hoped that it would make an exception in your case and come up with a solution that you were happy with. But even after my involvement you were given mixed messages about what was feasible. You were becoming more frustrated by the minute, so I asked Aviva what was going on. Eventually it agreed to let you set up a self-invested personal pension (Sipp) so you can withdraw payments as and when you like, which you were satisfied with.  Aviva said: “We are sorry that we did not contact our customer prior to his 75th birthday. We apologise for this error and the subsequent delays it caused. We have reviewed our processes to help ensure this doesn’t happen again.” It paid you £18,056 so that you’re not financially disadvantaged by the time that your pension was left in cash. This interest has been added to your pension of £110,542, giving you a total of £128,598, which has been transferred to your Sipp ready to be invested.  Aviva also gave you £500 compensation. Given that your ordeal had been going on for so long and caused you a huge amount of stress, neither you nor I were impressed with that figure, but it wouldn’t offer any more.  You said: “There was no end in sight until you intervened, so I want to say a very big thank you for getting in touch with Aviva on my behalf.”

articles.article_id: Aviva steers more pension savings into private markets
articles.article_filename: Aviva steers more pension savings into private markets.docx
articles.body: Aviva steers more pension savings into private markets New default option will allocate an initial 25 per cent of an employee’s pension pot to private equity, infrastructure and property  Millions of British pension savers could have up to 25 per cent of their pots handed to private market groups including KKR and Apollo Global to manage, as Aviva pushes ahead with plans to shift more client money into unlisted assets. Aviva, a big player in managing UK employers’ pension schemes, said it was launching a new “default” option for employers and employees that would produce far greater exposure to private assets. The new “My Future Vision” (MFV) option will allocate an initial 25 per cent of an employee’s pension pot to private equity, private credit, infrastructure and property. That compares to a 10 per cent allocation under its existing “My Future Focus” (MFF) default and 0 per cent under its low-cost “My Future” default.  Employers and employees will still be able to opt out, but in practice few tend to. Defaults are powerful “nudge” tools in determining the choices made by clients. Aviva expects the new default to lead to 5-6 per cent of people’s pots going into specifically UK-based private equity and infrastructure, which would align with the company’s recent promise under the Mansion House accord agreed with Rachel Reeves.  Aviva has assets of £419 billion and is one of Britain’s biggest managers of defined contribution (DC) pension schemes, including £15 billion for 800,000 people in its auto-enrolment master trust. Initially, the new default will be offered only to the £30 billion of trust-based schemes it manages but Mai Rajah, Aviva’s director of investments, said he hoped the same approach could one day be applied to contract-based schemes, when the rules allow it.  Members would benefit from higher risk-adjusted returns, he added. The trade-off because of a lack of liquidity and higher fees in private assets was “worthwhile”.  The new default, Rajah said, was “a major step forward for DC pension schemes when it comes to the scope and sophistication of private market investing”.  The shift to private assets is in full swing. Legal & General and Fidelity have also made moves to put more DC pension fund assets into the asset class, which has produced strong returns in the past few decades and improved diversification. As well as KKR and Apollo, Aviva has signed up Neuberger Berman, Stepstone and Invesco to manage the money. All five are headquartered in the US.  The government has argued that UK savers are missing out on higher eventual pensions because many are almost entirely invested in listed shares and bonds.  However, some experts are more cautious, arguing that the strong returns from private assets in the past may not be repeated in future, that the asset class is less liquid and less transparent and that savers will also have to shoulder much higher fees. Private credit in particular is an untested asset class, ballooning since 2008 when crisis-hit banks pulled back from much corporate lending.  The Mansion House pledge of many fund managers to allocate 5 per cent of pots to UK private assets has raised eyebrows with some, who argue ministers should not be pressuring the British public to take more potential risk when their own defined-benefit pensions are guaranteed in all circumstances. The asset allocation shift could also be seen as a fresh blow to listed markets, including the London Stock Exchange. While Aviva’s existing MFF default puts 90 per cent of a young earner’s contributions into listed shares, the new MFV default will allocate only 75 per cent.  Contract-based pension schemes are for now precluded from easily investing in private assets because they are bound by a fees cap of 0.75 per cent. For trust-based schemes, the cap excludes private assets. Neuberger and Stepstone will manage the private equity allocation, KKR will be in charge of infrastructure investment, Apollo and Stepstone will handle private credit and Invesco will manage the real estate assets. The in-house team at Aviva Investors will also be given some mandates.  The private markets allocation in the new default will start at 25 per cent for young employees and drop to 20 per cent by retirement age. Within the overall private markets bucket, 35 per cent will be allocated to private equity, 25 per cent each to private credit and infrastructure and 15 per cent to real estate. Asset class allocation, rather than the choice of individual stocks, has been much more important over the years in determining pot sizes and therefore retirement incomes.

articles.article_id: Aviva wins backing for plan to cancel
articles.article_filename: Aviva wins backing for plan to cancel.docx
articles.body: Aviva wins backing for plan to cancel ‘irredeemable’ shares Investors have been offered a premium to resolve the issue after an abortive attempt seven years ago  Investors have approved a plan by Aviva to cancel £450 million of supposedly “irredeemable” preference shares seven years after a City backlash forced the FTSE 100 insurer to abandon a previous attempt. Aviva provoked a furore in 2018 when it revealed it was looking at repurchasing the securities at par value, which would have resulted in big losses for investors because the shares were trading above that level.  The debacle contributed to the ousting of the company’s chief executive Mark Wilson later that year and resulted in the insurer subsequently being publicly censured by the Financial Conduct Authority over its handling of the affair.  Yet the issue of cancelling the preference shares, which were first issued in 1992 and 1993, has not gone away. Rule changes mean that from the beginning of next year, the securities will no longer count towards Aviva’s regulatory capital. The shares also cost Aviva about £38 million a year in coupon payments to investors. The insurer revived its plan to cancel them last month but, unlike last time, it is offering investors a premium to the shares’ market value.   Aviva also pledged not to cancel the securities without securing the specific approval of at least 75 per cent of voting preference shareholders, which it obtained on Tuesday during four investor meetings held to decide on the issue. While both ordinary and preference shareholders overwhelmingly backed Aviva’s plan at different votes, it was not without criticism.  Philip Meadowcroft, a holder of ordinary Aviva shares, told George Culmer, the insurer’s chairman, that “you have thrown irreedemability under the proverbial bus by replacing it with words like ‘cancelling’ and ‘retiring’.” He added: “Irredeemable, chairman, has to mean unchangeable — nothing more, nothing less.”  Culmer, who was not at Aviva during the attempt to tackle the issue in 2018, insisted the insurer was “not doing this in a perfunctory, unfair manner” and that it had always had the ability to cancel the securities.  “Yes, they were marketed as irredeemable, but yes, the world has moved on,” he said. Aviva’s ordinary shares were up 10¾p, or 2.1 per cent, at 522½p by the close on Tuesday.

articles.article_id: My wife has months to live but Aviva rejected our
articles.article_filename: My wife has months to live but Aviva rejected our.docx
articles.body: My wife has months to live but Aviva rejected our £20k life insurance claim Our consumer champion Katherine Denham helps a couple caught in a wrangle over a terminal illness payout Katherine Denham , Consumer champion Sunday May 18 2025, 12.00am, The Sunday Times  In 2019 my wife was diagnosed with terminal lung cancer and began taking medication to prevent it from spreading. Sadly this medication stopped working last year and the cancer has spread to her lymph nodes, spine, liver and breast. She had emergency surgery in October to remove a large tumour in her bowel and is about to start chemotherapy to try to slow down the progression of the cancer. We have a decreasing life insurance policy with the insurer Aviva and have been paying £20.46 a month since 2011. The policy also gives us a payout if either of us are diagnosed with a terminal illness. We first claimed in 2019 but Aviva rejected our claim because her doctor said she was likely to survive for more than 12 months. When it became clear that my wife’s medication was no longer working, we claimed again last September. We had a report from her oncologist saying that her life expectancy could be less than 12 months. We were shocked when Aviva finally responded in December to once again reject our claim. We appealed against this decision and Aviva apologised for the delay but said it was very busy due to an unprecedented level of demand on its resources.  We have complained to Aviva but it has been four months and we haven’t heard back. Our policy should give us about £20,000, which we would have used to have a more comfortable life. We also hoped to use some of the money to book a special family holiday which we could have cherished with our three daughters and two grandchildren. As my wife is now so unwell, I don’t think that’s possible any more.   We are struggling with her condition enough as it is, so this problem with Aviva just brings extra stress and pressure when we least need it. We just feel Aviva has been far too slow, especially considering my wife has just months left to live. Name and address supplied Katherine Denham writes  I was deeply saddened to hear of your wife’s illness and that you had been living with such uncertainty for so many years. It was completely understandable that you wanted the assurance of a payout to make things easier in her final months. Like many life insurance policies, yours offers an early payout before death if you’re diagnosed with a terminal illness and given less than 12 months to live. But there was another time limit to consider. Within 18 months of your policy’s expiration date, you can no longer claim for terminal illness, meaning that Aviva would only pay out on death. Your policy was due to expire in March next year, but thankfully you had raised your claim just before the 18-month window and so it was still valid. Considering that time was of the essence, it was incredibly disappointing that you hadn’t heard from Aviva for months. It told me that after you appealed its decision in December, it got another oncologist to review the medical information and look at the average life expectancy of others undergoing the same treatment.  That oncologist thought it was plausible that your wife could live for more than a year, although this depended on how she responded to treatment. Aviva should have sent you a letter explaining this but it failed to do so, which it blamed on human error. After I called the company it reviewed your case again. As it became clear that your wife’s treatment wasn’t working, it decided to pay out your claim based on its value in September, giving you £23,455. Aviva said: “We were very sorry to hear of our customer’s diagnosis and we understand the seriousness of her condition. We did review this again following the customer’s appeal in December but we regret that we have no record that our response was sent, for which we are extremely sorry. “With the new information recently provided that the customer is sadly no longer responding to treatment, we now have a clearer picture of her life expectancy. As this is now likely to be less than 12 months, we believe it is appropriate in these circumstances to pay out the remaining value of the policy.” It has given you a £500 goodwill payment and you said: “I cannot thank you enough, we are so grateful.”

articles.article_id: Pension funds could be forced to invest in UK projects
articles.article_filename: Pension funds could be forced to invest in UK projects.docx
articles.body: Pension funds could be forced to invest in UK projects The government is set to give itself the power to force investment in areas it regards as important if funds fail to comply with voluntary pact  The government is set to give itself the power to mandate pension funds to invest cash in UK projects if they fail to comply with a voluntary pact due to be announced this week.  A review, led by pensions minister Torsten Bell, is expected to recommend that the government is given the option to mandate pension fund investment, according to a source close to the government. The review will be published later this month after an agreement this week by more than 15 major pension fund investors — thought to include FTSE 100 giants L&G, Aviva, Phoenix and M&G — to invest up to 10 per cent of their assets in fast-growing companies and infrastructure, with half of that designated for the UK, by 2030.  Known as the Mansion House Accord, it will amount to at least £25 billion of extra investment from workplace pension funds.
