But it has exposed divisions in the City about whether forcing pension funds to invest in certain areas clashes with their “fiduciary duty”, which is to provide the best return to their investors. There has been speculation that the government will use a regulatory “backstop” to force investment in areas it regards as important.  One senior City source warned there was “growing concern” that it would lead to lower returns for pension funds.  In addition, a push to invest more in unlisted assets could be more expensive for pension fund holders. Fees charged by pension fund providers to invest in private markets are higher than those for investing in the stock market. The Accord is intended to build on the 2023 Mansion House Compact, under which pension funds agreed to invest 5 per cent of their assets in unlisted equities by 2030. It is being orchestrated by Alastair King, the lord mayor, a figurehead position at the City of London Corporation — the local authority for the financial district. A wide range of pension fund providers are said to have signed up to the Accord but it is not clear whether Scottish Widows, owned by Lloyds Banking Group, has done so. Scottish Widows is the biggest of the default fund providers for workplace pensions.  The Treasury and the City of London Corporation declined to comment.

articles.article_id: Risks of private markets deserve public scrutiny
articles.article_filename: Risks of private markets deserve public scrutiny.docx
articles.body: Risks of private markets deserve public scrutiny If Rachel Reeves wants pension pots to seek higher returns from sometimes opaque investments, the Treasury needs to take some interest in overseeing them Alistair Osborne Friday January 09 2026, 12.01am, The Times As fans of private markets go, few beat Rachel Reeves. She has so much faith in private equity, private credit, private real estate and other private stuff — venture capital, infrastructure funds and, as she put it, “exciting start-ups” — that she coerced 17 pension providers into the Mansion House Accord.  What that? A so-called “voluntary” agreement signed last May by the likes of Aviva, Legal & General and M&G to allocate “at least 10 per cent” of their default defined contribution pension funds into private assets by 2030, with 5 per cent of that going into the UK. In short, tens of billions of quid from our pensions pots. Still, what better than that for “delivering growth” and “giving working people greater security in retirement”, as the chancellor said at the time? And who knows, maybe Reeves will be proved right. Private markets have generally outperformed the public sort since the 2008 financial crisis. Yet, they also bring all sorts of risks, as a report headed “Private markets: unknown unknowns” from the House of Lords financial services regulation committee spelt out on Thursday. And the least you’d expect, given Reeves’s enthusiasm, is that the Treasury is fully across those risks. • Treasury ‘doesn’t appreciate’ potential risks of private markets boom Instead, here is what the committee, chaired by Lord Forsyth of Drumlean, found: “HM Treasury’s evidence demonstrated a limited grasp of the concerns raised during this inquiry, which suggested passivity in the face of potential risks to the UK’s financial stability arising from the growth of private markets.”  How reassuring is that? As the report relates, since 2008 these markets have taken off, with global assets under management quadrupling to $16 trillion — three-quarters in the US but with Britain in second place, presiding over $185 billion in the first quarter of 2025. The key reasons for the growth? Quantitative easing and low interest rates, which prompted investors to seek racier returns in private equity and private credit.  The problem? As it says on the tin, such markets are private, so it’s tricky to know precisely what’s going on — even if regulators are paying increasing attention. The lordships say there is “insufficient data” to conclude whether there’s systemic risk. But the Bank of England’s Financial Policy Committee identified three key features last July that could send things off in that direction: “The widespread use of leverage, the opacity of valuations and extent of reliance on rating agencies, and the correlation with other risky funding markets.”  In the private credit market, last September’s collapse of two US companies — car parts supplier First Brands and sub-prime auto lender Tricolor — ramped up fears over weak lending criteria. As the Bank of England governor, Andrew Bailey, asked at the time: “Are these cases idiosyncratic or are they what I call the canary in the coal mine?” As the report notes, too, we already have examples of the “interconnectedness” between private markets and the wider financial system: 2021’s collapse of hedge fund Archegos Capital Management, say, which caused $10 billion of losses to global banks.  Quantifying such risks in a world where the likes of private equity continuation funds meet AI-fuelled stock markets is tricky — not least when you add Trumpian political risk. But a Lords committee shouldn’t need to spell out to the Treasury that, with the taxpayer on the hook for any blow-up, it must take more “responsibility” for overseeing private markets rather than simply “delegating” it to regulators. Not least when Reeves wants more private stuff in our pension funds.

Tax on salary sacrifice ‘bad news’ for Britain Aviva chief urges Rachel Reeves to think again about capping the schemes in her budget, saying it would be bad for Britain  A tax raid on salary sacrifice schemes would be “bad news” for Britain because it would penalise employers and disincentive workers from saving for retirement, the boss of one of the country’s biggest pension providers has warned. Dame Amanda Blanc, the chief executive of Aviva, said on Thursday that “we would urge the chancellor to think very carefully about changes to salary sacrifice” in the forthcoming budget. Under the schemes, employees give up part of their pay in return for pension contributions and other benefits. The money comes out of wages before it is taxed and, by reducing salaries, means workers and employers pay less national insurance.  Currently, workers can put as much as £60,000 a year tax-free into pensions.  However, tax breaks for salary sacrifice are set to be curtailed by Rachel Reeves, with the chancellor expected to use her budget on November 26 to announce a cap of £2,000 a year on the amount that can be sacrificed without being subject to national insurance, which could raise £2 billion a year.  This would push up national insurance costs for employees and employers and has prompted warnings from pensions experts that companies are likely to respond by making less generous pension contributions. “What you’re effectively doing is penalising those employers that actually contribute more to employees’ pensions,” Blanc said. “But you’re also saying to people who save for their pension that perhaps they shouldn’t do it, and I think that’s bad news long-term for the UK if you think about the fact that 15 million people in the UK are not saving enough.”  It would represent another national insurance hit for companies, after Reeves used her first budget in October last year to increase employer contributions, which angered many bosses. Blanc warned: “The actual cost to employers of removing that NI benefit from salary sacrifice is not going to be insignificant.”  She was speaking as Aviva posted third-quarter results, in which the FTSE 100 insurance and savings group almost doubled the cost savings it expects to realise from its recent £3.7 billion takeover of the insurer Direct Line. It now expects to deliver £225 million of run-rate savings by 2028 from the deal, up from its previous forecast of £125 million. Aviva also said it expected to generate an operating profit of £2.2 billion this year before any contribution from Direct Line, meaning it will have reached its target of £2 billion by 2026, a year earlier than planned, and set new three-year goals for the business which include delivering a return on equity of more than 20 per cent by 2028.  Analysts at Bank of America reiterated their “buy” advice on Aviva’s stock to clients and told them: “We think it will beat its new targets.” However, Aviva shares slid 42½p, or 6.1 per cent, to close at 650p, having surged by about 40 per cent since the start of the year, with many of the target upgrades announced by the insurer on Thursday already anticipated by investors.

articles.article_id: UK’s biggest pension funds back regional growth drive
articles.article_filename: UK’s biggest pension funds back regional growth drive.docx
articles.body: UK’s biggest pension funds back regional growth drive Legal & General and the Universities Superannuation Scheme are among 20 members of a new investment partnership called Sterling 20  Affordable housing and rural broadband provision will be the first beneficiaries of a new regional investment drive by 20 of Britain’s biggest pension providers and insurers. Legal & General has made a £2 billion commitment to invest in delivering 10,000 affordable homes by 2030, while Nest is to invest £40 million in fibre broadband in Scotland and northern England, and provide £100 million to Schroders Capital to make UK investments.  • The latest business and markets news — follow live The companies are part of a new partnership called Sterling 20 that will be established at a first regional investment summit with Rachel Reeves in Birmingham this week.  The Treasury said the initiative was designed to “channel the nation’s savings into key infrastructure and fast-growing businesses in key modern industrial strategy sectors like AI and fintech”.  The 20 participants also include M&G, Phoenix, Aviva, Royal London and the Universities Superannuation Scheme.  The government is trying to encourage British pension funds to invest more in the UK and especially in growth industries and private businesses. “Even a small shift towards investing in UK infrastructure would unlock billions,” the Treasury said.  In May, the government co-ordinated the Mansion House accord in which 17 pension providers committed to invest at least 5 per cent of their main default funds in UK private markets. These 17 providers have all joined Sterling 20, alongside Rothesay and PIC, the annuity providers, and the Pension Protection Fund.  In the summer the government revamped the Office for Investment — a joint unit of the Treasury, business department and No 10 — to help encourage investment in Britain.  The Treasury said that the office’s “unique position as a bridge between government, investors and local leaders will allow it to match transformational investment opportunities with capital”. António Simões, chief executive of Legal & General, said: “Our £2 billion commitment, targeted at housing, infrastructure, and urban regeneration, will help unlock the investment needed in productive assets across the country — creating jobs, strengthening communities, and driving both regional and national growth.”  Ian Cornelius, chief executive of Nest, said the UK had “exceptional investment opportunities” and was “a cornerstone of our strategy”. He added: “We have already committed around £4 billion to UK private markets, and by 2030 we expect this to rise to around £12 billion. A strong pipeline of opportunities will be essential to realising this growth for the benefit of our members and the UK economy.” As part of the summit, the US infrastructure company Welltower will announce plans to more than triple investment in the UK to $12 billion over the next five to ten years.  An S&P 500 listed long-term investor specialising in the healthcare infrastructure sector, Welltower has already invested $4 billion in the UK over the last decade. It operates 299 social care properties across the UK, from Dorset to Scotland and supports a supply chain employing almost 28,000 people.  The company’s existing plans will add more than 2,500 extra beds to social care capacity, with 90 per cent of these outside of London. It comes amid a severe supply and demand imbalance for social care in Britain, which ministers are seeking to address.
